claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child receives qualifying benefits (e.g. UC, ESA) in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child receives qualifying benefits (e.g. UC, ESA) in their own right
circumstances: Child is 18 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child receives qualifying benefits (e.g. UC, ESA) in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let's start with the basics:

**How many children are you looking to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! I'm looking to claim Child Benefit for my child Alex, who is 18 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Alex.

Since Alex is 18 years old, eligibility for Child Benefit depends on whether they are in approved education or training. You can normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and stays in approved education or training.

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** 

For example, this would include:
- A-levels or equivalent
- Scottish Highers
- NVQs up to Level 3
- A course at a college or university that started before they turned 18

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is currently studying for A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great, thank you for confirming that Alex is studying A-levels at sixth form college. That counts as approved education, which is good news for your eligibility.

I have a few more questions to check the other requirements:

**Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you! Manchester is in the UK, so that's confirmed.

Now, regarding your responsibility for Alex:

**Does Alex live with you, or if not, do you contribute at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment (currently £25.60 per week for the eldest child) towards their upkeep, such as food, clothes, or pocket money?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent, thank you for confirming that.

Just a couple more questions to make sure there's nothing that would affect your eligibility:

**Does Alex do any paid work for 24 hours or more per week, or receive any benefits in their own right (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex doesn't work 24 hours a week or anything like that, but they do receive Universal Credit in their own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know that important detail.

Unfortunately, this does affect your eligibility. According to the Child Benefit rules, you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts getting certain benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit.

Since Alex receives Universal Credit in their own name, this would make you ineligible for Child Benefit for Alex, even though they are under 20 and in approved education.

Let me provide you with the formal eligibility outcome:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on my assessment, I'm sorry to say that **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

**Summary:**

| Child | Eligible? | Reason |
|-------|-----------|--------|
| Alex | No | Receives Universal Credit in their own name |

**Explanation:**

While you meet many of the requirements for Child Benefit — you live in the UK, Alex lives with you, and Alex is under 20 and in approved education — the fact that Alex receives Universal Credit in their own name disqualifies you from receiving Child Benefit for them.

If Alex's circumstances change in the future (for example, if they stop receiving Universal Credit while still in approved education), you may become eligible at that point and could make a new claim.

If you have any questions or believe your circumstances might change, you may wish to contact the Child Benefit Office for further guidance.

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old and in approved education (A-levels at sixth form college), which would normally allow the claimant to continue receiving Child Benefit for a child under 20. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester) and Alex lives with them, meaning they are responsible for the child. However, Alex receives Universal Credit in their own name. According to the rules, Child Benefit stops if a child starts getting certain benefits in their own right, including Universal Credit. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets most eligibility criteria for Child Benefit: they live in the UK, are responsible for Alex (who lives with them), and Alex is under 20 and in approved education. However, because Alex receives Universal Credit in their own name, this disqualifies the claimant from receiving Child Benefit for Alex, as the rules state that Child Benefit stops when a child starts receiving certain benefits such as Universal Credit.

According to the rules, Child Benefit stops if a child starts getting certain benefits in their own right, including Universal Credit. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

However, because Alex receives Universal Credit in their own name, this disqualifies the claimant from receiving Child Benefit for Alex, as the rules state that Child Benefit stops when a child starts receiving certain benefits such as Universal Credit.
